heroOverview: Necatoriasis is infection by Necator americanus, the New World hookworm and the dominant hookworm species globally, responsible for an estimated 70–80% of the world's approximately 472 million hookworm infections. Adult worms attach to the lining of the small intestine via cutting plates, removing 0.03–0.05 mL of blood per worm per day and causing chronic iron-deficiency anemia that accumulates silently over months to years of continuous infection. The species is endemic across tropical and subtropical sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, South and Southeast Asia, and Oceania, where warm humid soil sustains larval survival. Like its close relative Ancylostoma duodenale, Necator americanus is a neglected tropical disease (NTD) targeted by WHO mass drug administration programs. In children, chronic infection impairs growth and cognitive development; in pregnant women, it contributes to maternal and perinatal mortality. Anthelminthic treatment with benzimidazoles is safe and inexpensive, though single-dose cure rates against N. americanus are modestly lower than for A. duodenale.

keyStats.prevalence: Necator americanus accounts for 70–80% of an estimated 472 million global hookworm infections (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: School-age children and adults in rural, agricultural, and low-income tropical communities bear the highest burden
keyStats.avgAge: Highest worm burdens and morbidity in children aged 5–14; adults in endemic areas remain chronically infected
keyStats.globalCases: Soil-transmitted helminths together cause 1.5–5.1 million DALYs annually; hookworm contributes a disproportionate share due to blood-loss anemia

definition: Necatoriasis (ICD-10: B76.1) is intestinal infection caused by Necator americanus, a hematophagous (blood-feeding) nematode belonging to the family Ancylostomatidae. N. americanus is distinguished from Ancylostoma duodenale by its smaller size (5–11 mm), semi-lunar cutting plates rather than teeth, and predominance in the Americas, sub-Saharan Africa, and parts of Asia. The life cycle is strictly soil-dependent: eggs passed in feces hatch in warm, moist soil within 1–2 days; larvae develop through two free-living stages into infective filariform L3 larvae within 5–10 days, and actively penetrate human skin — characteristically the feet and lower legs when a person walks barefoot on contaminated ground. Unlike A. duodenale, N. americanus does not infect via the oral route and does not undergo hypobiosis (larval arrest), making the soil-to-skin pathway its exclusive route of transmission. After skin penetration, larvae enter the venous circulation, travel to the pulmonary capillaries, migrate through alveoli, ascend the bronchial tree, are swallowed, and mature into adults in the proximal small intestine within 5–9 weeks (prepatent period). Adult worms live 3–10 years and each female produces 5,000–10,000 eggs per day. The principal morbidity is iron-deficiency anemia and hypoalbuminemia from chronic blood loss; blood loss per worm (0.03–0.05 mL/day) is approximately four- to six-fold lower than A. duodenale, so higher worm burdens are generally required to produce equivalent anemia. Necatoriasis is a WHO-listed neglected tropical disease targeted by preventive chemotherapy programs with albendazole and mebendazole.

typesClassification.description: A pruritic, erythematous, maculopapular or vesicular eruption at larval entry sites — typically the feet — within hours to 2 days of skin contact with infective larvae in contaminated soil.
Larvae traversing pulmonary capillaries and alveoli produce transient cough, mild wheeze, and eosinophilic pulmonary infiltrates (Löffler-like syndrome) lasting 1–2 weeks; typically mild.

typesClassification.type: Established intestinal infection — light burden (< 40 worms)
typesClassification.description: Minimal or no symptoms; may produce mild eosinophilia and slight reduction in iron stores without clinically apparent anemia.

typesClassification.type: Established intestinal infection — moderate to heavy burden (≥ 40–100+ worms)
typesClassification.description: Progressive iron-deficiency anemia, hypoalbuminemia, abdominal discomfort, and nutritional impairment; this is the stage responsible for the bulk of necatoriasis morbidity.

primarySymptoms: Intense itching and localized papular or vesicular rash at the skin entry site (ground itch), typically on the foot or ankle, appearing within hours to days of barefoot soil contact in an endemic area.
Chronic iron-deficiency anemia producing fatigue, pallor, weakness, dizziness, and exertional breathlessness — the dominant and most clinically significant consequence of established infection.
Epigastric, periumbilical, or diffuse abdominal pain from adult worms attached to the intestinal mucosa, often described as gnawing or vague cramping.
Anorexia, nausea, and intermittent loose stools or diarrhea during active intestinal infection.
Peripheral lower-limb edema from hypoalbuminemia (protein loss into the gut) in heavy chronic infection.
Impaired physical growth and underweight in children with heavy longstanding infection — reflecting the combined burden of anemia, iron depletion, and protein-calorie loss.
Reduced cognitive function and poor school performance in infected children, attributable to iron-deficiency effects on brain development and myelination.
Pica (craving for earth, clay, or other non-food substances) associated with iron-deficiency states in some endemic populations.
Transient cough and wheeze during pulmonary larval migration, typically self-limiting over 1–2 weeks.
Signs of high-output cardiac compensation — tachycardia, cardiac flow murmur — in severe chronic anemia from heavy worm burdens.
earlyWarningSigns: Itchy, raised rash on the foot or lower leg within hours to 2 days of barefoot contact with soil in a tropical or subtropical region.
Mild unexplained cough and fatigue appearing 1–2 weeks after exposure in an endemic area, coinciding with the pulmonary migration phase.
Gradual onset of pallor and increasing tiredness over weeks to months without an obvious dietary cause in a person from or returning from an endemic region.
Mild abdominal discomfort and anorexia persisting for more than a few days in a traveler or migrant who may have had barefoot soil contact.
emergencySigns: Hemoglobin below 7 g/dL with signs of cardiac decompensation — tachycardia, dyspnea at rest, and bilateral leg edema.
Tarry black stools (melena) indicating significant gastrointestinal blood loss from heavy worm attachment.
Acute breathlessness or pulmonary infiltrates during the larval migration phase requiring urgent respiratory assessment.
Confusion or syncope from critically low hemoglobin, necessitating emergency blood transfusion evaluation.
Severe bilateral edema with respiratory symptoms suggesting acute high-output heart failure from profound anemia.

causes.cause: Skin penetration by infective L3 larvae in contaminated soil
causes.description: The sole transmission route for N. americanus: infective filariform larvae actively migrate to the soil surface in warm, moist conditions and penetrate the skin, particularly the interdigital spaces and soles of the feet. Infection requires soil temperatures of at least 15–18°C and adequate moisture, restricting endemic transmission to tropical and subtropical zones.

causes.cause: Fecal contamination of peridomestic and agricultural soil
causes.description: Open defecation and the use of untreated human waste as crop fertilizer maintain high egg and larval concentrations in soil around homes and agricultural fields. Communities without adequate sanitation infrastructure sustain continuous transmission cycles.

causes.cause: Barefoot contact with agricultural, forest, and peridomestic soil
causes.description: Farmers, children at play, and communities that routinely go barefoot sustain the highest exposure to infective larvae. Sandal-wearing communities in the same region have substantially lower infection rates, directly demonstrating the causal pathway.

causes.cause: Inadequate hand washing after soil contact
causes.description: Though the oral route is not confirmed for N. americanus, hands contaminated with soil after gardening or field work can carry larvae to the mouth when people eat without washing, potentially contributing to incidental ingestion.

Necator americanus thrives where annual mean temperatures exceed 20°C and rainfall sustains year-round soil moisture; climate change-driven range expansion may increase transmission in currently marginal zones. Endemic hotspots include rural sub-Saharan Africa, South and Southeast Asia, and tropical Latin America.

riskFactors.description: The primary modifiable risk: wearing footwear consistently in hookworm-endemic areas eliminates soil-to-skin transmission. Even sandals substantially reduce larval exposure.
Necatoriasis is endemic wherever warm temperature, soil moisture, and open defecation coexist; prevalence often exceeds 50% in rural communities in affected regions.

riskFactors.factor: Agricultural occupation with sustained soil contact
riskFactors.description: Farmers, plantation workers, and miners in endemic areas are in direct soil contact for many hours daily; prevalence among adult agricultural workers in highly endemic zones can approach 80%.

Children accumulate the heaviest worm burdens through barefoot outdoor play; the consequences of iron-deficiency anemia and protein malnutrition during critical developmental periods produce long-lasting educational and physical deficits.

Pregnant women with N. americanus infection have amplified iron requirements, making even moderate worm burdens sufficient to produce clinically significant anemia; WHO recommends deworming in the second trimester in endemic countries.
Individuals already deficient in iron or protein experience more severe clinical manifestations from the same worm burden; nutritional supplementation is an integral part of management.
HIV infection and severe malnutrition impair immune-mediated worm expulsion, potentially allowing heavier infections to establish and persist.

affectedDemographics: School-age children (5–14 years) in tropical regions — the priority group for WHO mass deworming programs
Pregnant women in endemic countries, targeted for preventive chemotherapy from the second trimester
Agricultural and subsistence-farming communities across sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and Latin America
Preschool children (1–4 years) in highly endemic areas, who can accumulate significant worm burdens once they begin walking outdoors
Travelers and migrants returning from rural tropical regions, especially those who walked barefoot or worked in soil
diagnosisOverview: Necatoriasis is diagnosed by microscopically identifying hookworm eggs in stool. The Kato-Katz thick-smear technique is the WHO standard: it quantifies eggs per gram (epg) of feces and classifies infection intensity as light (< 2,000 epg), moderate (2,000–3,999 epg), or heavy (≥ 4,000 epg). Single-examination sensitivity is approximately 60–70%; examining three separate stool specimens raises sensitivity above 90%. Hookworm eggs (55–75 × 35–40 μm, thin-shelled, with a 4–8 cell morula) are morphologically identical for N. americanus and A. duodenale, so species-level identification requires Harada-Mori larval culture (examining L3 morphology) or PCR-based methods — useful for epidemiological purposes but rarely required clinically. Formalin-ethyl acetate sedimentation is an alternative concentration technique used in reference laboratories in non-endemic settings. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is most pronounced during the larval migration phase (weeks 1–4 of infection) and may be modest or absent in chronic intestinal infection. Full blood count revealing microcytic hypochromic anemia, combined with low serum ferritin and reduced transferrin saturation, quantifies the iron-deficiency burden and guides supplementation therapy. Stool occult blood is frequently positive with moderate-to-heavy worm loads. Important differential diagnoses include other intestinal nematode infections (Ascaris, Trichuris, Strongyloides), dietary iron deficiency, gastrointestinal blood loss from other causes, and hemoglobin disorders such as thalassemia.

diagnosticTests.purpose: WHO-recommended quantitative method; identifies and counts hookworm eggs per gram of stool to classify infection intensity as light, moderate, or heavy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Provide a fresh stool sample in a clean container. A thick smear is prepared, stained, and read under a microscope. Single-specimen sensitivity is 60–70%; three specimens over separate days are optimal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid, low-cost point-of-care method to detect hookworm eggs in settings without Kato-Katz materials; less sensitive for light infections.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small smear of fresh stool on a glass slide with saline solution, examined under a microscope within 30 minutes. Widely used in field settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Concentrates eggs and larvae from stool, increasing sensitivity for light infections common in travelers; standard in reference labs in non-endemic countries.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A preserved stool aliquot is processed in the laboratory and examined under a microscope; results available in 1–2 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with differential and reticulocyte count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of iron-deficiency anemia (microcytic, hypochromic) and eosinophilia; the reticulocyte count reflects bone marrow response to iron deficiency or therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results within a few hours. Eosinophilia is most prominent during larval migration; anemia severity guides iron supplementation dosing.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum ferritin, iron, and transferrin saturation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms iron-deficiency status and quantifies the depletion, guiding the intensity and duration of iron supplementation alongside anthelmintic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood test; ferritin below 12 μg/L is the most sensitive marker. Total iron-binding capacity and transferrin saturation complete the picture of iron status.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates N. americanus from A. duodenale by culturing L3 larvae from fresh stool and examining morphology; used in research and epidemiological work.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Stool on filter paper is incubated at 25°C for 7–10 days. Not routinely done in clinical management of individuals.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) for hookworm species
diagnosticTests.purpose: Species-level molecular identification from stool or larval culture; most sensitive method and useful in low-intensity infections and research settings.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: DNA is extracted from a stool sample and run in a laboratory PCR assay; results in 1–3 days from a specialized laboratory.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of necatoriasis combines anthelminthic therapy with iron and nutritional repletion. Albendazole 400 mg as a single oral dose is the WHO-recommended first-line agent for all soil-transmitted helminths including N. americanus; however, single-dose cure rates against N. americanus are lower than for A. duodenale — approximately 45–72% in systematic reviews — so a three-day course (albendazole 400 mg daily for 3 days) is preferred for heavy infections and is increasingly recommended for all individual treatment in clinical settings. Mebendazole 500 mg as a single dose is an alternative, with similar single-dose efficacy limitations for Necator; a 3-day mebendazole regimen (100 mg twice daily for 3 days) improves cure rates. Both agents inhibit tubulin polymerization, causing energy depletion and worm death. Iron supplementation is equally essential and should begin simultaneously with anthelminthics: ferrous sulfate 200 mg (60 mg elemental iron) two to three times daily, continued for 3–6 months based on ferritin recovery. Vitamin C co-administration enhances non-heme iron absorption. Severely anemic patients (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL or < 8–9 g/dL in pregnancy) may need blood transfusion before iron stores can be rebuilt. Protein and caloric supplementation addresses hypoalbuminemia in severe chronic infections. Follow-up stool microscopy 2–4 weeks after treatment confirms egg reduction or cure. In endemic communities, WHO-endorsed preventive chemotherapy programs administer annual or twice-yearly doses to school-age children, preschool children, and pregnant women, effectively controlling rather than eliminating community burden.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole (single or 3-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line anthelminthic for necatoriasis; 400 mg single dose is used in mass drug administration programs and has cure rates of approximately 45–72% for N. americanus. A 3-day course (400 mg daily for 3 days) increases cure rates to approximately 80–90% and is preferred in clinical individual-patient treatment, particularly for heavy infections. Taken with food to improve bioavailability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure 45–72%; 3-day course cure approximately 80–90% based on systematic review data.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole (single or 3-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative benzimidazole; 500 mg single dose or 100 mg twice daily for 3 days. Single-dose cure rates against Necator are 15–60% in trials; the 3-day regimen approaches 70–80% cure. Poorly absorbed, limiting efficacy against larval stages and systemic infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure highly variable (15–60%); 3-day regimen achieves approximately 70–80% cure for N. americanus.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral iron supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Ferrous sulfate, ferrous fumarate, or ferrous gluconate providing 60 mg elemental iron two to three times daily alongside anthelmintic therapy, continued until ferritin normalizes (typically 3–6 months). Vitamin C co-ingestion enhances absorption. Side effects (nausea, constipation) are managed with dose reduction or formulation change.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Raises hemoglobin by approximately 1–2 g/dL per month; essential for full clinical recovery alongside anthelminthics — anthelmintics alone do not reverse established iron-deficiency anemia.

medicalTreatments.name: Blood transfusion
medicalTreatments.description: Packed red blood cells administered for severe symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL with hemodynamic compromise, or lower thresholds in pregnant women and young children). Stabilizes the patient while oral iron and anthelmintics address the underlying cause.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each unit of packed red cells raises hemoglobin approximately 1 g/dL in an average adult; rapid correction of severe anemia prevents cardiac decompensation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Shoe-wearing and environmental sanitation
alternativeTreatments.description: These structural interventions prevent reinfection and break the transmission cycle. In controlled community studies, consistent shoe-wearing has been shown to reduce hookworm prevalence by 50–90% in previously endemic communities. Anthelmintic treatment without environmental change leads to rapid reinfection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Therapeutic helminth administration (research context)
alternativeTreatments.description: Controlled human infection trials with N. americanus larvae are being explored as an immune-regulatory treatment for inflammatory bowel disease and celiac disease, based on observations that hookworm infection suppresses Th2 allergic and inflammatory responses. This remains experimental and is not a clinical therapeutic option.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of necatoriasis can be managed by a general practitioner or primary care physician, particularly in endemic settings where the diagnosis and treatment protocols are routine. Specialist care from an infectious disease or tropical medicine physician is warranted for severe iron-deficiency anemia (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL), treatment failure after two appropriate anthelminthic courses, pregnant women in early gestation requiring individualized risk-benefit assessment, immunocompromised patients, and non-endemic patients in whom differential diagnosis is broader. Hematology referral is appropriate for anemia requiring parenteral iron or transfusion.
doctorSelectionGuide: In endemic regions, community health workers, nurses, and general practitioners trained in WHO deworming protocols can manage routine cases. In non-endemic settings — North America, Europe, Australia — a travel medicine clinic, infectious disease specialist, or tropical medicine program is the appropriate resource. Look for clinicians with access to parasitology laboratory stool analysis. For pediatric patients with growth delay, a pediatrician or pediatric infectious disease specialist familiar with NTD management is ideal. Ask about the centre's approach to iron supplementation monitoring and reinfection prevention counseling.
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes or sandals consistently in all tropical and subtropical environments, particularly when walking on moist soil or in gardens, farms, or forests.
Use latrines or toilets and promote open-defecation-free communities to prevent egg contamination of soil.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before eating and after soil contact.
WHO preventive chemotherapy programs recommend annual or biannual albendazole or mebendazole for pre-school children, school-age children, and pregnant women (from second trimester) in endemic areas.
Nutritional supplementation with iron and folic acid for pregnant women and children in endemic regions, independent of deworming, to reduce baseline iron-deficiency risk.
Avoid using untreated human feces (night soil) as agricultural fertilizer; composting at adequate temperature substantially reduces infective larval density.
lifestyleModifications: Wear footwear at all times in hookworm-endemic areas; this single behavior change provides near-complete protection against skin-penetration transmission.
Complete the full 3-day course of albendazole when prescribed for individual treatment, and take all doses with food to maximize absorption.
Continue iron supplementation for the prescribed 3–6 months; stopping early because fatigue improves risks leaving iron stores depleted.
Re-test stool 2–4 weeks after completing anthelmintic treatment to confirm egg clearance; arrange retreatment if positive.
In endemic communities, participate in school-based and community deworming programs on the schedule offered.
Encourage children to wash feet and wear shoes to school and during outdoor play to interrupt the highest-risk exposure.

recommended: Iron-rich foods eaten at every meal: red meat, organ meat (liver), legumes, dark leafy greens (spinach, amaranth)
Vitamin C foods (citrus, papaya, guava, tomatoes) consumed alongside plant-source iron to maximize absorption
Protein-rich foods (eggs, fish, legumes, dairy) to address hypoalbuminemia in chronic heavy infection
Iron-fortified foods (fortified flour, rice, cereals) available as cost-effective sources in low-income settings
avoid: Tea, coffee, and dairy products at the same time as iron-rich foods or iron supplements — polyphenols and calcium inhibit iron absorption
Raw or unwashed vegetables grown in hookworm-endemic soil
High-phytate foods (unfermented bran, raw legumes) eaten alone without enhancers such as vitamin C

exerciseGuidelines: During active symptomatic infection with significant anemia, strenuous physical activity should be limited because the cardiovascular system may be unable to meet increased oxygen demand. Gentle movement is appropriate for mild infections. As hemoglobin rises with iron therapy — typically over 4–8 weeks — activity can be progressively restored to normal. Children who have been treated should return to normal physical activity and school participation as soon as hemoglobin begins to recover, which itself promotes appetite and nutritional intake.
prognosis: Necatoriasis carries an excellent prognosis when treated. Anthelminthic therapy eliminates most worms, and 3–6 months of iron supplementation restores hemoglobin and iron stores fully. In children, recovery from anemia is associated with measurable improvements in physical growth, working memory, and school attendance. Because adult N. americanus worms live 3–10 years, untreated infections persist for years without reinoculation, producing progressively worsening anemia; early treatment therefore prevents significant long-term morbidity. In endemic settings, reinfection after treatment is universal without behavioral and environmental change, which is why annual or biannual preventive chemotherapy is required to sustain the gains of any single deworming campaign. Maternal and neonatal outcomes are substantially improved when hookworm infection is treated during pregnancy. Case fatality from uncomplicated necatoriasis is extremely low, occurring mainly in settings with no access to medical care where severe anemia progresses to high-output heart failure.
recoveryTimeline: Ground itch and pulmonary migration symptoms resolve spontaneously within 1–2 weeks of exposure, regardless of treatment. Following anthelmintic therapy, eggs disappear from stool within 2–4 weeks. Hemoglobin rises at approximately 1–2 g/dL per month with consistent iron supplementation; moderate anemia (hemoglobin 8–10 g/dL) typically normalizes within 2–3 months. Serum ferritin replenishment requires 3–6 months. In children with growth faltering, catch-up growth may continue for 6–12 months after the anemia is corrected. Eosinophilia normalizes within 1–3 months of successful treatment.
complications: Iron-deficiency anemia — potentially severe (hemoglobin < 7 g/dL) in heavy infections; the principal complication and cause of most hookworm-related morbidity and mortality globally.
High-output heart failure — profound chronic anemia forces the heart to increase cardiac output, eventually causing cardiomegaly, flow murmurs, and in extreme cases, decompensated heart failure.
Hypoalbuminemia and protein-energy malnutrition — chronic enteric protein loss and reduced appetite produce edema, poor immunity, and delayed wound healing in heavy sustained infections.
Intrauterine growth restriction, preterm birth, and neonatal mortality — hookworm anemia during pregnancy reduces oxygen and nutrient supply to the fetus, with consequences extending beyond birth.

Cognitive impairment and educational underachievement — iron-deficiency anemia during brain development impairs myelination, working memory, and processing speed; effects may persist even after successful treatment.

Löffler-like eosinophilic pneumonia — transient pulmonary infiltrates with cough and wheeze during larval lung migration; self-limiting but can be severe in sensitized individuals with heavy re-exposure.

supportResources.name: CDC — Hookworm
supportResources.description: Clinician and patient resource on hookworm biology, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: Neglected Tropical Diseases Coalition
supportResources.description: Advocacy and education resource on neglected tropical diseases including hookworm, with links to programme data and patient resources.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ancylostomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus produce clinically indistinguishable disease; A. duodenale causes greater blood loss per worm, can infect via the oral and transmammary routes, and predominates in the Mediterranean and South Asia, while N. americanus is the global dominant species in the Americas and sub-Saharan Africa. Species identification requires larval culture or PCR.

confusedWithConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Strongyloides stercoralis also penetrates skin and causes eosinophilia and intestinal symptoms, but is distinguished by its capacity for autoinfection and decades-long persistence, hyperinfection syndrome in immunosuppressed patients, and the presence of larvae (not just eggs) in stool.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ascariasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ascaris lumbricoides also causes eosinophilia and pulmonary migration symptoms but produces large visible roundworms in stool, does not cause iron-deficiency anemia at low burdens, and cannot penetrate skin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Schistosoma species also produce eosinophilia and anemia but are transmitted through fresh water contact, not soil, and primarily target the liver, vasculature, and bladder; they are diagnosed by characteristic eggs in stool or urine.

relatedConditions.name: Ancylostomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The closest related condition — A. duodenale hookworm infection producing identical clinical disease via the same soil-transmission mechanism, treated with the same agents.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Whipworm infection routinely co-occurs with hookworm in children in endemic settings; both are targeted by WHO mass drug administration programs with benzimidazoles.

relatedConditions.name: Ascariasis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most prevalent soil-transmitted nematode; polyparasitism with N. americanus is the norm in endemic communities and both respond to benzimidazole treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Strongyloidiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another skin-penetrating nematode with overlapping geographic distribution and epidemiology; both are NTDs that may co-infect the same patients.

relatedConditions.name: Helminthiasis (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The broad category representing poly-helminth infections, which is the typical pattern in hookworm-endemic settings where multiple species co-exist.

faqs.question: What is necatoriasis and how is it transmitted?
faqs.answer: Necatoriasis is infection by the hookworm Necator americanus, the most common hookworm worldwide. Infective larvae live in warm, moist soil contaminated by human feces. They penetrate bare skin — typically the feet — travel through the bloodstream to the lungs, are swallowed, and grow into adults in the small intestine, where they attach and feed on blood.

faqs.question: What symptoms does Necator americanus hookworm cause?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is chronic iron-deficiency anemia causing fatigue, pallor, weakness, and breathlessness from daily blood loss by the worms. Earlier symptoms include a localized itchy rash on the foot at the skin entry site and transient cough during larval migration through the lungs. Heavy infections also cause abdominal pain, anorexia, and leg swelling.

faqs.question: How is necatoriasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is made by examining a stool sample for hookworm eggs under a microscope using the Kato-Katz technique. Blood tests typically show raised eosinophils and a microcytic anemia with low iron (ferritin). Species identification between Necator and Ancylostoma requires larval culture or PCR and is rarely needed in routine clinical care.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for necatoriasis?
faqs.answer: Albendazole 400 mg for 3 days is the preferred individual-patient treatment for Necator americanus, as single-dose cure rates are lower for this species than for Ancylostoma. Iron supplementation — typically for 3–6 months — is essential alongside anthelmintics to correct the anemia caused by blood loss.

faqs.question: Why are single-dose cure rates lower for Necator americanus than for Ancylostoma duodenale?
faqs.answer: Necator americanus appears to be somewhat less susceptible to the single recommended dose of albendazole or mebendazole than Ancylostoma duodenale. Single-dose cure rates in trials range from 45–72% for N. americanus compared with 72–95% for A. duodenale. A 3-day course raises N. americanus cure rates to approximately 80–90% and is preferred in individual clinical treatment.

faqs.question: Is Necator americanus the same as Ancylostoma duodenale?
faqs.answer: Both are hookworms causing virtually identical clinical disease, but they are distinct species. Necator americanus is the dominant hookworm globally and removes less blood per worm than A. duodenale. It cannot infect via the oral or breast-milk routes, unlike A. duodenale. They are transmitted the same way — through bare skin contact with contaminated soil.

faqs.question: Can hookworm infection cause anemia?
faqs.answer: Yes, iron-deficiency anemia is the defining complication of hookworm infection. Each adult Necator americanus worm removes approximately 0.03–0.05 mL of blood per day. Heavy infections with hundreds of worms cause significant chronic blood loss, eventually depleting iron stores and lowering hemoglobin. Treatment requires both anthelmintics to kill the worms and iron supplements to rebuild stores.

faqs.question: How do I prevent necatoriasis?
faqs.answer: Wearing shoes in tropical regions is the single most effective individual prevention. Use latrines to prevent soil contamination, wash hands before eating, and wash vegetables grown in soil. In endemic countries, WHO-endorsed deworming programs provide annual or twice-yearly anthelmintic treatment to children and pregnant women.

faqs.question: Can necatoriasis affect pregnant women?
faqs.answer: Hookworm infection during pregnancy significantly increases the risk of severe maternal anemia, which in turn raises the chances of low birth weight, preterm delivery, and poor fetal development. WHO recommends anthelminthic treatment from the second trimester in endemic countries, combined with iron and folic acid supplementation throughout pregnancy.

faqs.question: What is ground itch in hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Ground itch is the itchy, red, raised skin rash that appears at the site where hookworm larvae have penetrated the skin — usually on the foot or between the toes. It develops within hours to days of exposure and typically resolves within 1–2 weeks as the larvae migrate away from the skin. It is the first sign of new hookworm infection.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for necatoriasis take to work?
faqs.answer: Anthelmintic treatment eliminates most worms, and eggs disappear from stool within 2–4 weeks. The anemia takes longer to resolve: hemoglobin rises at approximately 1–2 g/dL per month with iron supplementation, so moderate anemia may take 2–3 months to normalize. Full iron store replenishment requires 3–6 months of supplementation.

faqs.question: Is necatoriasis contagious between people?
faqs.answer: No. Necatoriasis cannot spread directly from person to person. It requires a soil-based stage: hookworm eggs passed in feces need 5–10 days in warm moist soil to develop into infective larvae. A person with the infection is not infectious to household contacts through touch, coughing, or shared surfaces.

faqs.question: Can children get serious complications from hookworm?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children bear the greatest burden from hookworm because they accumulate the heaviest worm loads and are at critical developmental stages. Severe iron-deficiency anemia impairs brain development, myelination, and working memory. Chronic infection causes stunted growth and poor school performance. These effects are at least partly reversible with timely treatment.

faqs.question: Does hookworm affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: During the larval migration phase, Necator americanus larvae pass through pulmonary capillaries and alveoli, causing a transient cough and mild wheeze called Löffler syndrome. This phase lasts about 1–2 weeks and is self-limiting in most people. Severe lung symptoms during this phase are uncommon.

faqs.question: How is hookworm different from tapeworm?
faqs.answer: Hookworm (Necator americanus) is a small round nematode that penetrates the skin, lives in the small intestine, and causes blood-loss anemia. Tapeworm (Taenia species) is a flat segmented worm acquired by eating undercooked meat; it attaches to the intestinal wall and causes mainly weight loss and digestive symptoms. They are unrelated parasites treated with different medications.

faqs.question: Will I get reinfected after treatment?
faqs.answer: In endemic areas, reinfection is very common because Necator americanus larvae remain in the soil environment. Without changes in footwear habits and sanitation, reinfection can occur within weeks to months. This is why WHO recommends annual preventive deworming in high-risk communities rather than relying solely on single-treatment cure.

faqs.question: What blood test results suggest hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Key findings include elevated eosinophils on the white blood cell differential — most pronounced during larval migration — and microcytic hypochromic anemia with low serum ferritin and low transferrin saturation. Stool microscopy is needed to confirm the diagnosis; blood tests alone are not sufficient.

faqs.question: Can shoes really prevent hookworm infection?
faqs.answer: Yes, consistently wearing shoes is one of the most effective individual preventive measures. Studies in endemic communities have shown that barefoot walking is strongly associated with hookworm infection and that shoe-wearing, even simple sandals, substantially reduces the risk of skin penetration by infective larvae in contaminated soil.

faqs.question: What specialist manages necatoriasis?
faqs.answer: Routine cases are managed by primary care physicians or general practitioners. An infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician is consulted for severe anemia, treatment failure, or diagnosis in non-endemic settings. Hematology may be involved for severe iron-deficiency anemia. Pregnant women with hookworm benefit from co-management with an obstetrician.

metaDescription: Necatoriasis is a hookworm infection causing iron-deficiency anemia, skin rash and fatigue. Learn how Necator americanus is diagnosed and treated.
